sections.title: Basic Sentence Structure
sections.content:
In toki pona, every simple sentence follows the pattern: subject + li + predicate. The particle 'li' separates the subject from the predicate (verb or adjective). However, when the subject is exactly 'mi' or exactly 'sina', li is dropped.

Examples:
- jan li toki. (A person speaks.)
- soweli li lape. (The animal sleeps.)
- mi moku. (I eat.) — no li after mi
- sina pona. (You are good.) — no li after sina

callouts.text: Drop li only when the complete subject is exactly 'mi' or 'sina' — not 'mi en sina' or 'mi mute'.
mi mute li toki. (We speak.) — li is kept here because the subject is 'mi mute', not bare 'mi'.

sections.title: Direct Objects with e
sections.content:
To add a direct object to a sentence, use the particle 'e' between the verb and the object. The e particle marks exactly what the action is done to.

Structure: subject (+ li) + verb + e + object

Examples:
- mi moku e kili. (I eat fruit.)
- ona li lukin e tomo. (She looks at the house.)
- sina jo e ilo. (You have a tool.)

callouts.text: You can chain multiple objects: mi moku e kili e pan. (I eat fruit and bread.)
Adjectives and prepositions never follow e — only nouns (or noun phrases) do.

sections.title: Modifiers and Adjectives
sections.content:
In toki pona, modifiers (adjectives, adverbs) always come after the word they modify. This is opposite to English. Multiple modifiers can be stacked.

Examples:
- jan pona (good person, friend)
- tomo suli (big house)
- telo pona mute (very good water / a lot of good water)

The particle 'pi' regroups modifiers. Without pi, all modifiers attach directly to the first noun. With pi, the words after pi form a phrase that modifies the first noun as a group.

- jan pi toki pona (toki pona person — a person of toki pona)
- jan toki pona (good-language person — ambiguous stacking)

callouts.text: Use pi when you have a two-word modifier: jan pi ma ante (person of a different land).
Never use pi with a single word — pi pona is wrong. Just use pona directly.

sections.title: Pre-verbs (Modal Verbs)
sections.content:
Pre-verbs come before the main verb and change its meaning. Common pre-verbs include:
- wile (want to, need to)
- ken (can, may)
- kama (become, manage to)
- awen (keep on, continue to)
- sona (know how to)
- lukin (try to)
- open (start to)
- pini (stop, finish)

Structure: subject (+ li) + pre-verb + main verb

Examples:
- mi wile moku. (I want to eat.)
- sina ken toki. (You can speak.)
- ona li kama sona e toki pona. (She is learning toki pona.)

callouts.text: Pre-verbs can be stacked: mi wile ken moku. (I want to be able to eat.)

sections.title: Context Clauses with la
sections.content:
The particle 'la' marks the end of a context clause that precedes the main sentence. The context clause can express:
- Time: tenpo suno la, mi pali. (In the daytime, I work.)
- Condition: sina pona la, mi pilin pona. (If you are good, I feel good.)
- Topic: toki pona la, nimi lili li mute ala. (As for toki pona, there aren't many words.)

Structure: [context] la [main sentence]

callouts.text: The context before la can be a full sentence or just a noun phrase.
mi lape la, o kalama ala. (While I sleep, don't make noise.)

sections.title: Prepositions: lon, tawa, tan, kepeken
sections.content:
Toki pona has four main prepositions that attach to the end of a sentence:
- lon: at, in, on (location or existence)
- tawa: to, for, moving to
- tan: from, because of
- kepeken: using, with

Examples:
- mi moku lon tomo. (I eat in the house.)
- mi tawa ma pona. (I go to the good land.)
- ona li kama tan ma ante. (She comes from another land.)
- mi pali kepeken ilo. (I work using a tool.)

callouts.text: lon, tawa, tan, and kepeken can also be used as verbs: mi lon. (I exist / I am here.)

comparisons.title: li vs no li (with mi/sina)
comparisons.explanation: When the subject is exactly 'mi' or 'sina' alone, drop li. For all other subjects — including 'mi mute' — keep li.

comparisons.title: Modifier order: toki pona vs pona toki
comparisons.left: toki pona (good language / toki pona language)
comparisons.explanation: Modifiers follow the word they modify. toki pona means a language that is good (or the specific language). Reversed order pona toki would mean a goodness that is language-like, which is rarely used.

comparisons.title: e (direct object) vs lon (location)
comparisons.left: mi lukin e tomo. (I look at the house.)
comparisons.right: mi lon tomo. (I am in the house.)
comparisons.explanation: e marks a direct object — the thing directly acted upon. lon is a preposition indicating location or existence. Don't use e with prepositions.

comparisons.title: pi (grouping) vs stacked modifiers
comparisons.left: jan pi tomo pona (person of the good house)
comparisons.right: jan tomo pona (good-house person — can be ambiguous)
comparisons.explanation: Without pi, all modifiers attach one by one to the head noun. pi groups subsequent words as a unit that modifies the head noun.

comparisons.title: wile (want/need) as pre-verb vs noun
comparisons.left: mi wile moku. (I want to eat.)
comparisons.right: mi jo e wile moku. (I have a desire to eat.)
comparisons.explanation: As a pre-verb, wile precedes the main verb and means to want/need to do that action. As a noun (with e before it), wile refers to the desire itself.

quiz.question: What does 'e' mark in a toki pona sentence?
How do you say 'I want to sleep' in toki pona?
What does 'pi' do in a toki pona noun phrase?
Which sentence means 'She works in the house'?
When do you drop 'li' from a sentence?
options: When the sentence is a question
When the subject is exactly 'mi' or 'sina' alone
When the verb is a pre-verb

quiz.question: What does 'la' introduce in a sentence?
